text: After bilateral uterine artery coagulation, the surgeon inserts a myoma screw and uses a monopolar device to make a sharp incision at the most prominent part of the fibroid along the posterior uterine wall.
The surgeon uses a monopolar instrument to enucleate a deep intramural fibroid from its bed, revealing the fibroid's position within the uterine wall.
The surgeon uses a monopolar instrument to dissect the fibroid out of its capsule, carefully separating the fibroid from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon applies gentle traction and counter traction on the fibroid using a myoma screw, facilitating its manipulation within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a monopolar device to continue the dissection, enucleating the fibroid from its vascular bed, with visible separation of the fibroid from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a monopolar device to remove fine attachments at the fibroid's bed, with minimal bleeding observed due to prior bilateral uterine artery coagulation.
The surgeon sutures the fibroid capsule back in multiple layers using a barbed suture, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon places deep sutures within the surgical site, securing the tissue layers after fibroid enucleation.
The surgeon uses a suprapubic port and two needle holders to efficiently perform deep sutures through the myoma bed, minimizing the risk of hematoma formation.
The surgeon pulls the barbed suture out while applying gentle traction, visibly closing the cavity and the myoma bed.
The visual shows minimal bleeding through the vascular bed due to prior uterine artery coagulation, with the surgical field appearing relatively bloodless.
The surgeon uses barbed sutures to close the myoma cavity in a continuous fashion, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon uses interrupted barbed sutures to completely close the cavity of the myoma bed, ensuring secure closure and minimizing the risk of hematoma formation.
The surgeon uses a monopolar instrument to make a sharp cut at the level of the myoma, ensuring a smooth approximation of the uterine serosa.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the uterine cavity, with minimal bleeding observed due to prior uterine artery coagulation.
The surgeon uses a morcellator to extract the fibroid from the peritoneal cavity, visibly removing the tissue from the surgical field.
The surgeon takes an extra stitch at the end of the suturing with the barb, ensuring the stitch interlocks with the previous stitches for secure closure.
The surgeon enucleates the fibroid from the cavity, using the opposite port as a guide to form long strips of tissue, which are then extracted from the peritoneal cavity.
